title: You Can Have the Crown
original_prose:
“Well, they call me King Turd up here on Shit Mountain / If you want it you can have the crown”

— Sturgill Simpson, "You Can Have the Crown"



####

SYNRGY INTERNAL MEMORANDUMCLASSIFICATION: Level Omega / Eyes-Only / Vessel Management ProtocolsSUBJECT: RT-Class Vessel Yield Optimization – Maternal Containment Guidelines

Effective immediately, all reproductive-tier vessels (RT-V) designated for Spark harvest are to remain in a fixed-yield cycle without deviation. Medical sedation is authorized only during active Spark capture. No further accommodations for vessel comfort, consent, or recovery are necessary under revised compliance law (Ref. Reclamation Act 77-F).

Clarification: The term woman holds no operational value within SynRgy framework architecture. Personnel are advised to reference RT-Vs solely by ID tags or as "biological containers." Gendered identifiers are considered Signal noise and may trigger doctrinal recalibration.

Waste flesh from post-yield collapse is to be routed through standard biomass reclamation chutes. Secondary Spark potential (SP2) is to be evaluated within six minutes of core cessation. Any residual warmth shall be classified as grace latency and extracted accordingly.

Violation of these guidelines will result in conversion or disciplinary yield.

There is no burden in obedience. There is only Spark.— Office of Sanctified Logistics, Division 9: Maternal Throughput



####

The hallway was half-lit and sweating steam. Everything smelled like burnt coolant and birthing fluid. Somewhere, someone was still screaming, but it didn’t sound close enough to care.

Kat moved like the air owed her something. Low, quick, sharp. I followed. Not because I had a plan—but because I didn’t want to break the rhythm. We moved like we’d been trained together. Like we’d bled in the same patterns before.

Two guards turned the corner. Neither of them clocked us fast enough. One had his helmet off, still mid-laugh. Kat didn’t wait.

Her blade flashed. Neck, gone. Blood sprayed across the wall like a signature.

The second reached for his sidearm. I moved with him. Parried up. Slammed him into the wall hard enough to crack something inside. Then I drove my sword through his gut and left it there a second too long.

He gurgled. Died slow. That was on me.

We stood over the bodies. Steam rising off the floor. Kat’s coat slipped down her shoulder again. She didn’t fix it. Didn’t care.

We stood over them. Breathing. Listening.

“We fight like we been doin’ this a while,” Kat said.

“Maybe we have.”

She nudged one with her toe, then crouched. Took the sidearm and a handful of bloody armor pieces—shoulder plate, thigh wrap. Buckled them quick over bare skin. Refused the boots.

"Ain’t never could stand shoes," she muttered. I wiped blood and sweat from my eyes. Same texture, different sins.

“We’re not gonna make it out like this.”

She glanced at me. Waiting.

“They’re not coming for me,” I said. “They’re coming for you. And they’ll send a lot more next time.”

“You sayin’ I’m special?”

“I’m sayin’ they think you are.”

She looked down the corridor. Sirens shifting tone. Getting closer.

“So what’s the play, Captain America?”

I pointed.

Steel hatch. Recessed. Big enough to drive a coffin through. Someone had scrawled in grease or blood—maybe both:

mEaT cHuTe

“You out your damn mind,” she muttered.

“You said you were open to suggestions.”

“Yeah. Anythin’ but that.”

“Straight drop to the sublevels. No guards. No scanners. Just slide and pray.”

“Like tryin’ to pick a restaurant with your crazy-ass ex.”

I almost laughed.

Then came the boots. Too many. Fast.

“We don’t have time.”

She stared at the hatch. Then at me.

“You first.”

“Why me?”

“If it eats you, I pick somethin’ else.”

I grabbed the wheel. It fought back, then gave. Heat spilled out—wet and copper-bright.

“I hate you already,” she said.

I jumped.



####

I didn’t fall. I slid.

The chute gripped me—not tight, but greedy. Like it had waited too long for something warm. The walls were slick. Viscous. Not metal, not quite. Something in between steel and scar tissue. It pulsed.

I hit a bend and spun sideways. Shoulders scraped. My sword caught and screamed across the surface, a shriek swallowed by the curve of the descent.

Kat followed. I heard her swear. Then something heavier hit just after her. A noise like wet laundry and bone.

The chute wasn’t empty.

It had layers.

Above us, vents exhaled bursts of static and sound. A voice filtered down—half lullaby, half customer service rep.

"Compliance is kindness. Yield is joy. Your Spark is the Light of the World."

The walls pulsed harder.

Another body slid past me—limp, headless. It bumped my leg on the way down and didn’t apologize. I twisted, braced, gritted my teeth. Kat howled from somewhere above me. A mix between laughter and disgust.

“This some bullshit, baby!”

She sounded closer.

“I told you!” I yelled.

Another body joined. Smaller. Infant-sized. It hit my chest and bounced off. I didn’t have time to feel anything about it. I just screamed and kept sliding.

The propaganda got louder.

"Thank you for your offering. Spark clarity has been achieved."

We were being thanked. That was the worst part.

Something wet smacked into my back. I didn’t look.

We spun again. The light changed—shifting from red to white to something like sickly mint.

Kat’s voice reached me again:

“You better hope we live through this. ‘Cause if we don’t, I’m hauntin’ your narrow ass!”

I didn’t answer.

We were almost there.

I could feel the end coming.

Something caught me.

Not a wall. Not a body. Some knot in the chute—half-mechanical, half-muscled—grabbed the hilt of my sword and yanked. My momentum died. My spine screamed. I twisted, jammed one shoulder up, and got stuck.

I hung there, breath ragged. Arms pinned. Couldn’t move. Couldn’t scream. Everything stank like sweet rot and silicone.

Then—slap.

Kat.

She slammed into me, slick and swearing, knees and elbows bouncing off my ribs.

“Get your damn sword out the way!”

Before I could answer, she slid lower, hit resistance, and wedged face-first into mine.

We froze.

Nose to nose. Filth and blood between us. Her coat barely holding on, one shoulder bare, smeared in whatever this chute coughed up. Eyes bright with madness.

And yeah—she was pressed against me. All of her. No question about any of it. It wasn’t the first time I’d noticed. But now it was just us and gravity and skin. I didn’t flinch. I didn’t lie to myself.

She was Kat. That was all.

Her skin was dark and lustrous, even here—coated in gore and refracted in red light. Her honey-colored eyes locked on mine. That Creole defiance, that swamp-born fire—none of it dulled. If anything, the blood made her glow harder.

She looked like she belonged here—like the blood bowed to her.

She grinned.

Then, like it was the most natural thing in the world, she reached down and cupped me through the gore-slick fabric.

“Still workin’, I see.”

My breath hitched. Not from shame. Just surprise. Not the moment for it. Not the place. But she didn’t care. That was the point.

And then she kissed me.

Hard.

Nothing gentle. Nothing poetic. Just mouths and heat and pressure in a place meant for corpses. I felt every inch of her—raw, real, alive.

Then she pulled back.

"Told you I’d haunt you,” she said, laughing.

And then she was gone. Slid right past me, wild and barefoot and grinning like this was her idea of a date night.

I wrenched my sword free and followed.



####

We hit wrong.

Not clean, not cinematic. Just bodies and metal and sound. I landed on something soft that cracked. Maybe a ribcage. Maybe a skull. Kat was gone—swallowed in the heap. I couldn’t see her.

I clawed upward. Hands slipping on limbs and rot. Elbows. Knees. The corpse slurry sucked at me like wet sand with teeth.

Then I saw her. Half-covered. Rising.

A hiss. Not hers. From above.

No alarm. Just pressure. A valve screamed open.

The corpse-wash.

A jet of fluid slammed down like God’s own firehose, meant to blast the meat clean before pickup. Blood peeled from bone. Muck stripped from the dead. But Kat—she stood. Head tilted back. Arms half-raised.

She was still naked. Still covered in half the dead. But in that moment—steam rising, coat gone, body cut with bruises and brilliance—she looked like resurrection. Not the soft kind. The kind that judges.

Her body was carved from something older than rules. Her skin, slick with blood and fluid, caught the light like obsidian. Small breasts, firm and perfect. A cock that hung soft but proud, framed in steam and dripping gore like something sacred. Her hips were scarred, her thighs streaked red, her eyes gold as honey and just as bright. She was everything they tried to erase—and she stood there unbent.

I felt the shame of arousal. The guilt of still being flesh. My cock pulsed against my thigh like it hadn’t gotten the memo about time or place. But I didn’t look away. I couldn’t.

Because she was glorious.

And she was mine, if only for a breath.

She caught my eye and smirked. One hand shielding her face. The other flipping the whole goddamn world off.

That’s when the guards finally noticed.

Three of them. White. Clean. Guns already up.

Too late.

Kat reached for the rifle she’d kept slung over her shoulder—barely shielded by the meat. It came up smooth, like she was drawing breath.

She fired. Twice. Center mass. Another to the throat.

I jumped the third.

Sword across neck. One motion. Didn’t even scream.

The workers didn’t move.

They just stared. Brown. Black. Uniformed in gray. Every one of them had the same expression: silent calculation. Waiting to see what would happen next.

Kat turned. Still soaked. Still breathing.

She didn’t yell. Didn’t preach. Just looked at them—one by one. Her voice came steady. Clean.

"I know what they done to y’all. Know what they still doin’. Some of you were born under this ceiling. Some dragged here in chains dressed up like jobs. But all of you know what this is. You ain't blind. You ain't dumb."

She took a breath. Not for effect. Just because she needed air.

"You don’t need to fight today. Don’t need to burn the place down. But you can see it. You can remember. And when the time comes, you turn your head the right way, and you help who you can. That’s how we win this shit—little cracks. Enough of ‘em and the whole wall comes down."

No one spoke. No one moved.

Then something shifted.

The workers—maybe a dozen of them—stepped together in quiet rhythm. No orders. No panic. Just a motion toward each other, like gravity had changed. They joined hands. Eyes closed. Heads bowed.

Not to Voss. Not to the Signal. To whatever god they remembered from before the machine. A god with breath and dirt and mercy in its bones.

They were praying.

I felt it in my chest before I understood it. They knew they were going to die. And they were still choosing their end.

Then the driver stepped forward.

Black. Gray in his beard. Eyes like driftwood. He handed her the keys.

No words. Just the act.

Kat took them slow. Like they were holy.

I watched it happen and couldn’t breathe. Not because of the gesture—because of what it meant. That man would die for this. They’d cut him down like he never existed, harvest his soul, and eat what was left. And he did it anyway. All of them knew they'd die just for the crime of seeing her. They'd die before they could even mention them to other workers.

Because she was worth it. Not me. Not the uniform I still bled in. Her.

She turned to me.

“You know how to drive this thing?”

“Yeah,” I said. “But it’ll drive itself once I line it up and give it the command.”

“Good,” she said, nodding to the rear. “Get us pointed. I'll get whatever ammo they have left. And another gun. You know how to shoot, Captain America, or are you only good with pointy things?"

"I know how to shoot."

She crouched beside the dead guards, rummaging ammo, slinging the rifle over her shoulder again.

"Wish I had twenty extra mags," she muttered. She ran her hand across the SOCOM Scout like it was a prayer wheel. "And maybe a couple severed heads in the bug-out bag."

I blinked. “I’m sorry, what?”

She didn’t answer. Just grinned.

"And I do love a glorious exit."



####

The truck lurched as I fed the command into the dash and punched the override. It slammed through the bay doors with a screech of steel and torque.

Electric power doesn't growl like diesel—it snapped. The tires howled as we fishtailed into the service alley, rear swinging wide, Kat bracing barefoot against the dashboard.

"You said you could drive," she shouted.

"I can drive," I said. "It's twitchy."

Kat fiddled with the dash. The interface snarled at her in Signal-script, warning about unauthorized media access.

"You're not gonna like SynRgy radio," I said.

Too late. The speakers hissed, and then—

Cindy Sheer’s voice. Honey-slick. Ghost-bright. Singing about Voss like he was a holy lover instead of a butcher.

He rose from flame, our digital king / With arms of light and a crown of string...

Kat made a noise like she was about to throw up in her mouth.

"Oh, I know," she said, grinning. "Just wanted to confirm it still sucked."

She spun the dial. Static. Screeches. A burst of coded prayer. Then—music. Real music. Gritty. Loud. Human.

"Man, it ain't me / I ain't no fortunate son..."

Blam. That wasn’t it. She kept scanning. And then it hit:

"They're sendin' soldiers to distant lands / Just to have them come home in a box / Turn off the TV, turn off the news / And let somebody think for you..."

She whooped, turned it up. The speakers buzzed with freedom.

"What the hell is this?" I asked.

"Radio Free USA," she said. "Pirate broadcast. One of the last ones still aimed at the Strip. Y'all too busy praising Spark Jesus to notice."

I blinked. I’d never heard it before. Never even knew it existed.

"They been bouncing this off satellites they ain’t even supposed to have. Old rebels. Drifters. Real humans. The kind that still bleed."

Another line tore through the speakers:

"Time to step aside or get out the way / It's a call to arms, boys, not a time to pray..."

She looked over, hair whipping in the wind, eyes wild.

"You can kill a soldier, baby. But a song?"

She tapped her temple.

"A song gets in people’s bones."

We whipped onto the main strip. Neon pulsed like an artery split open. The Strip wasn't empty. It was packed. Shoulder to shoulder—tourists, workers, sinners, drones. Faces blinking in the glitchlight. Every one of them turned to look.

First pursuit hit us a minute later. Two black trucks, armored, fast, maybe four guards in each. Then the drones.

"Keep it between the lines," Kat said, rolling down her window.

She climbed out without waiting for me. Rifle slung, eyes locked, still gloriously naked. I aimed the truck toward the same main gate I'd come through the first time—where I killed Eli. I set the auto-nav.

I climbed out behind her. Wind howled. Tires shrieked beneath me. But all I could see—all I could feel—was her.

She stood at the top like she’d been carved out of vengeance and starfire. Naked. Slick with sweat and the leftover shine of the corpse-wash. A little dirty. A little divine. Hair lashing in the wind. Eyes burning like gospel.

Not a woman. Not just a rebel.

A war goddess.

And I fell in love again.

Not the soft kind. Not the slow ache of memory or regret. This was the kind that sears. That brands. That cuts you open and says look—this is what holiness looks like now.

Every part of her said no. No to erasure. No to obedience. No to men like me who once thought we were saving the world by killing it.

She didn’t need to say a word. Her body was the sermon.

And the Strip? It was her congregation.

People froze. Gawked. Filmed. Cheered. Fell to their knees. I saw a woman pull her child close and point—look, baby, look. I saw an old man drop his cane like he'd just remembered how to stand. Someone screamed it's her, like they’d been waiting their whole life to say it.

But not everyone stared.

The Citizens—the SynRgy faithful, the clean-suited, compliance-coded faithful—they looked away. Flinched. Shielded their eyes like she'd burned a hole in their doctrine. Because they knew what it meant to see something like this. A naked trans woman with a gun and no shame? That's a conversion offense. That's a Spark harvest waiting to happen.

But the workers?

The workers couldn't look away.

Brown. Black. Tired. Owned. Every one of them marked by yield metrics and recycled uniforms. They saw her and stared. Because they had nothing left to lose and had never seen anything so fucking beautiful.

Not just the body. The defiance.

She was everything they were told to fear—and everything they needed to believe in.

Some cheered. Some cried. A few just stood there, mouths open, watching the world tilt sideways.

And then the feed went live. Every screen. Every billboard. Kat, naked and armed, standing in blood and steel.

The world didn’t just watch.

It witnessed.

The drone came in faster. Sleeker. A twin-blade model with a skin-slicing hum. Kat spun, crouched low on the roof, and fired three times without breathing. It dropped in sparks and fire.

Behind us, the black trucks were gaining. One veered onto the sidewalk, crunching a neon-lit bench and scattering bystanders. A second rammed through a vendor cart, sending hot dogs and blood in opposite directions.

Kat pivoted, rifle shouldered, and dropped a driver clean through the windshield. The truck flipped sideways, hit a post, and rolled.

"That one’s for the cafeteria," she muttered.

I grabbed a bloated torso, yanked it free of the pile, and lobbed it like a shot put. It hit the second truck’s grille, burst on impact, and sprayed the viewports. The driver panicked. Skidded.

Another drone dove low. Kat ran out of ammo. She didn’t blink—just vaulted toward it, grabbed a piece of rebar from the edge of the truck, and skewered the drone in midair like she was born to it.

The crowd screamed. Not in fear. In awe.

Then something changed.

The sky rippled. Like the Signal had been peeled back. A shape dropped onto the truck.

Not metal.

Not human.

WRAITH.

It looked like a man once. Maybe. But that had been scrubbed out by blade and doctrine.

Seven feet tall, wrapped in SynRgy’s second-skin—a shimmering mesh of bio-metal and Spark-absorbing coils. Its limbs bent too far the wrong way. The knees reversed. The spine arched like something mechanical that remembered having a soul. Joints hissed steam and flexed like wet knives.

Its face was no face. Just a mask of moving metal, mouthless, eyeless, with thin silver glyphs scrolling across its crown like scripture on repeat. You didn’t see its eyes—you felt them.

It made no sound.

But the air around it screamed. A low keening buzz, like a choir of feedback and mourning doves, broken into fractal noise. Not music. Not voice. Just Signal.

The sword on its back wasn’t forged—it was grown. From tech and hate. The same alloy used to cut open gods.

Its core pulsed visibly beneath its ribcage—a trembling orb of liquid light, like a newborn sun drowning in static.

And it hated her.

Every inch of its stance said: you are an abomination. Every ounce of its weight leaned toward the kill.

It was what SynRgy sent when bullets weren’t holy enough.

It didn’t speak. Just attacked.

Kat spun. Duck. Slash. She was fast, but it was faster. The edge of its blade caught her ribs. Blood sprayed. She screamed, but didn’t fall.

I dove, sword drawn. The WRAITH parried me like I was nothing. Knocked me back across the roof. My head rang. Everything tilted.

It turned back to her.

And that’s when she smiled.

She rolled, grabbed the fallen Scout rifle, jammed it into the WRAITH’s hip joint—and pulled the trigger.

Click.

Empty.

She grinned anyway. The WRAITH hesitated. That was enough.

She followed it with a kick to the face. Then another.

"You wanna see what a real god bleeds like?"

She screamed it loud enough to split the sky.

The blade she carried wasn’t hers—not really. She’d taken it off a guard back in the hallway. Standard issue SynRgy steel, curved at the belly, etched with compliance glyphs like obedience could be forged into metal. Every soldier carried one. Like they were Roman centurions. Like they were holy.

Kat made it holy.

She drew it now, one hand blood-slick, the other steady. Spun it once. Tested its weight. Then met the WRAITH head-on.

Steel met nano-fiber.

They fought like titans. Every blow was thunder. Sparks and blood and stripped circuitry. She ducked under a swipe, came up slicing. One arm gone. The crowd erupted. Even some of the SynRgy citizens. No one loved the WRAITHS. Not even me, and I commanded the damn things.

I wanted to cheer with them, but I couldn’t. Not because I was stunned—because I was busy. Drones kept coming. Black trucks too. Pursuers fanned out across the strip like roaches flushed from prayer.

So I did what I could. What I was good at.

I grabbed limbs, torsos, ruined helmets—whatever meat the bin had to offer—and turned it into artillery.

A headless corpse bounced off a drone’s intake and jammed the rotor. A thigh bone shattered a windshield. A spine wrapped itself around a pursuit cam and tore it from the air.

I fought off one of the guards who’d leapt onto the truck—jammed a broken elbow into his throat and kicked him into traffic. Another tried to climb up the rear. I dropped a pelvis on him. Watched him vanish under the wheels.

All the while, I could hear them—Kat and the WRAITH—tearing at each other just behind me. Every clash sent shockwaves down the steel. Every scream twisted the sky. But I didn’t look.

I couldn’t.

I knew better.

Some things aren’t meant to be seen head-on.

Some gods demand the corner of your eye.

The WRAITH roared. Charged. She didn’t flinch. Spun, drove her blade through its chest. Stuck.

It grabbed her throat.

It lifted her. One-handed. The WRAITH rose from the pile of bodies like death being born, blade still buried in its gut, Kat dangling from its fist.

For a moment, it looked like it was going to win.

But Kat wasn't done.

She bent her knees, planted both feet square against its chest—and pushed.

Her body arched backwards as she shoved off, ripping free, flipping in the air like a spark-wreathed angel. Mid-spin, she brought the blade with her—clean through its neck.

The head came off in a burst of sparks and fluid. She caught it mid-air.

Landed hard. One knee. One breath.

The head in her fist.

The body stood there a moment longer. Shuddering. Core pulsing like it didn’t understand what had happened.

She stepped forward. Drove the blade into its chest. Buried it to the hilt.

Then kicked it off the truck.

The crowd screamed. Cried. Cheered. Some ran. Some knelt. They were all about to die.

Kat lifted the head high.

And roared.

She looked at the WRAITH’s head—still twitching, still bleeding static—and blew it a kiss.

“Bless your heart,” she whispered.

Then she tossed it into the crowd.

Someone caught it.

A heartbeat.

The body behind us—what was left of it—twitched. Hissed. Began to pulse.

"Kat—"

"I know."

We both scrambled back into the cab. I hit the dash. Override. Emergency egress. All of it.

The truck swerved. Rocketed forward. We hit the gate like a fist through glass. Sirens split open behind us.

Then the WRAITH exploded.

The blast lit the sky. Consumed a block. People screamed. Fires bloomed.

The mirror caught the blast behind us. One block. Then two. Fire blooming like it remembered how.

Kat didn’t flinch.

She leaned back, one leg on the dash, smoke in her eyes.

"Told you I love a glorious exit."



Book Two - The Passage
